event: Bantu-speaking and Austronesian seafaring peoples begin settling the Comoro archipelago, including Mayotte, establishing the region's earliest known inhabitants.
Swahili trading communities develop along Mayotte's coasts, linking the island to the Indian Ocean commerce in gold, ivory, mangrove timber, and slaves.
Dembeni, one of the oldest archaeological settlements on Mayotte, flourishes as a center of trade and ironworking.
Islam takes root in Mayotte as Arab and Swahili merchants spread the faith throughout the Comoros archipelago.
Mayotte emerges as one of several Comorian sultanates, ruled by local Muslim sovereigns and integrated into Swahili trade networks.
Portuguese navigators sight and record the Comoro Islands, marking the first sustained European contact with the archipelago.
Mayotte's sultanate is repeatedly raided by Malagasy and Comorian pirates and slavers, destabilizing the island.
Andriantsoly, a deposed Sakalava king from Madagascar, seizes control of Mayotte and rules as its sultan.
Sultan Andriantsoly cedes Mayotte to France in exchange for an annual pension, making it France's first foothold in the Comoros.
France formally takes possession of Mayotte and abolishes slavery on the island, recruiting indentured laborers for new plantations.
Slavery is officially ended across Mayotte's plantations under French administration, transforming the island's economy.
Cyclones and falling sugar prices devastate Mayotte's plantation economy, prompting a shift toward other cash crops like vanilla and ylang-ylang.
Mayotte and the other Comoro Islands are administratively attached to the French colony of Madagascar.
The Comoros, including Mayotte, are formally made a province of Madagascar under French colonial rule.
The Comoros, with Mayotte, are detached from Madagascar and made a French overseas territory with its own administration.
The capital of the Comoros territory is moved from Dzaoudzi in Mayotte to Moroni on Grande Comore, fueling Mahorais grievances.
The Comoros gain internal autonomy within the French Republic, with growing political divergence over Mayotte's status.
In a self-determination referendum, the Comoros vote for independence, but Mayotte votes overwhelmingly to remain French.
The Comoros unilaterally declare independence, while Mayotte refuses to join and stays under French control.
Two referendums confirm that Mayotte wishes to remain part of France, and the island is given the status of a special collectivity.
The United Nations and African Union begin a long-running dispute, with Comoros claiming sovereignty over Mayotte as one of its islands.
France introduces the 'Balladur visa,' restricting movement between the Comoros and Mayotte and spurring dangerous clandestine sea crossings.
A referendum approves a new status for Mayotte as a 'departmental collectivity,' moving it closer to mainland French institutions.
Mahorais vote by an overwhelming margin (about 95 percent) in a referendum to become a full French overseas department.
Mayotte officially becomes the 101st department of France, deepening its integration into the French Republic.
Mayotte becomes an outermost region of the European Union, gaining access to EU structural funds and regulations.
Mass strikes and protests paralyze Mayotte over insecurity, illegal immigration, and poor public services, exposing deep social tensions.
France launches 'Operation Wuambushu,' a controversial crackdown on undocumented migration and shantytowns in Mayotte.
Cyclone Chido strikes Mayotte in December, causing catastrophic destruction and becoming the deadliest storm to hit the territory in decades.
Mayotte begins large-scale reconstruction after Cyclone Chido as France pledges a special recovery and development plan for the department.
